summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – ENFORCEMENT – Application for enforcement – Where final property orders were made in 2024 – Where the respondents failed to make payment to the wife as required under the final orders – Where the final orders contain default sale provisions – Where the husband and second respondent failed to cooperate with the default sale process – Where the wife seeks to be appointed attorney to conduct default sales – Wife appointed as attorney for default sale of properties FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Asset preservation order – Where the wife seeks the continuation of existing asset preservation orders pending payment of her entitlements under final property orders – Where the husband and second respondent oppose the wife’s application – Where the wife alleges that the husband and/or second respondent dealt with property without notice and in breach of final property orders – Consideration of principles related to interlocutory injunctions – Prima facie case established – Real risk of dissipation of assets – Application granted FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Where the wife seeks indemnity costs against the respondents in relation to applications made to enforce final property orders – Where final property orders were made some 16 months prior – Where the husband and second respondent engaged in conduct to frustrate satisfaction of the final property orders – Consideration of s 117 factors – Where the conduct of the husband and second respondent is exceptional – Costs awarded on an indemnity basis in the wife’s favour

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

By Amended Application in a Proceeding filed 2 December 2025 the applicant wife, Ms Genesalio, sought orders to aid the enforcement of her entitlements under the final property orders made by consent on 30 August 2024 ("the Final Orders").
That application came before the Court for mention on 5 December 2025. That day, orders were made by consent that the matter be listed for hearing on 4 February 2026 (“the adjourned hearing”). Further orders were made by consent that until 4.00pm on the adjourned hearing date, the second and fifth respondents be restrained from transferring or further encumbering their interests in the property at 1 D Street Suburb C (“[D Street]”) without the wife’s prior written consent or Court order.
In addition, orders were made by the Court that until the adjourned hearing date:-
The second and fourth respondents be restrained from transferring or further encumbering their interests in the property at L Street, Suburb C (“[L Street]”);
Each of the first to third respondents be restrained from transferring or further encumbering their interests in the property at H Street, Elwood (“[H Street]”); and
The respondents be restrained from disposing of, dealing with or diminishing the value of assets held by them in Australia up to $4,600,000;

without the wife’s prior written consent or Court order.

Directions were made for the preparation for the adjourned hearing, including directions that the respondents file and serve any material relied upon by them by 23 January 2026.
At the time those orders were made, the solicitor for the second respondent, Mr Franzese, expressed to the Court optimism that the matter would resolve without the necessity of a further hearing, it being his client's position that he was in the process of obtaining finance to enable satisfaction of the wife's entitlements under the Final Orders.
Notwithstanding that optimism, the proceedings were not resolved by the adjourned date. Accordingly, the wife pressed her application to enforce her entitlements under the Final Orders. In particular, the wife sought orders that:-
She be appointed attorney for each of the respondents to enable the conduct of the default sales provided by Orders 9 and 10 of the Final Orders;
The respondents be restrained from transferring or encumbering their interests in the properties at D Street, L Street, and H Street pending payment in full to the wife of her entitlements pursuant to the Final Orders; and
The respondents be jointly and severally liable to pay the wife's costs of and incidental to the enforcement application and the application previously made by her under s106A of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”).
The husband, Mr Genesalio, filed no response in respect of the wife's application. He was represented by his solicitor at the hearing, who confirmed that the husband would consent to orders in the terms of orders 11, 12 and 17 of the wife's amended application, (appointing her as attorney for the default sales). However, counsel for the husband confirmed that this consent was provisional upon those orders being self-executing and coming into effect only in the event that the husband had not complied with his obligations pursuant to the Final Orders regarding the default sales within seven days. That position was not agreed to by the wife.
The husband's solicitor confirmed that his client opposed orders restraining the transfer or encumbrance of the properties at D Street and L Street on the basis that those properties were not referred to in the Final Orders and further that they are not matrimonial property, but rather properties held by the second respondent. It was also submitted on behalf of the husband that he opposed an order for costs on an indemnity basis.
Other than stating the husband's position, the solicitor for the husband made no further submission in support of those matters.
The second respondent, being the husband’s brother, Mr M Genesalio, was represented by counsel who confirmed that his client opposed the wife's application and sought that it be dismissed. In his Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed 2 December 2025 the second respondent also sought orders that the Court set aside the Final Orders in order to “avert a miscarriage of justice” on the basis that the Final Orders were made “contrary to the intention of the parties”.
At the conclusion of the hearing, I made orders for the preservation of assets pending the wife's receipt of payment of her entitlements pursuant to the Final Orders. Those restraints included injunctions against the respondents from dealing with the properties at D Street, L Street and H Street. I also made orders that the wife be appointed as attorney for the conduct of the default sales provided for by the Final Orders.
At the time those orders were made I indicated to the parties that I would provide written reasons for judgment in relation to those matters together with my decision in relation to the wife's application for costs. These are those Reasons for Judgment.

BACKGROUND

This matter has had an extensive history before the Court which is detailed in the earlier judgments of Genesalio & Genesalio (No 2) [2023] FedCFamC1F 611 and Genesalio & Genesalio (No 6) [2025] FedCFamC1F 450. That history is also referred to in the decision of the High Court of Australia (Gageler CJ, Steward and Jagot JJ) in Genesalio v Justice Johns and Other Judges of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1) (2025) 426 ALR 160 (“[Genesalio]”) which dealt with the application by the husband and the second respondent for a constitutional or other writ and related application for a stay of the Final Orders.
I do not propose to repeat the history recorded in those judgments here.

MATERIAL RELIED UPON

The wife relied upon the following material
(a) Amended Application in a Proceeding filed 2 December 2025;

(b) Affidavit of Mr CC filed 10 October 2025;

(c) Affidavit of Mr CC filed 4 November 2025;

(d) Affidavit of Mr CC affirmed 2 December 2025;

(e) Affidavit of Mr CC filed 30 January 2026;

(f) Written Submissions filed 4 December 2025; and

(g) Written Submissions filed 30 January 2026.

The husband filed no material in response to the wife's application.
The second respondent relied upon the following material:
(a) Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed 2 December 2025;

(b) Affidavit of Mr M Genesalio affirmed 28 November 2025;

(c) Affidavit of Mr M Genesalio filed 3 February 2026; and

(d) Affidavit of Mr M Genesalio filed 28 January 2026.

In addition to those documents, on the afternoon before the hearing, the second respondent filed an affidavit containing further submissions. Those submissions were forwarded by the second respondent to the Court. I confirmed at the hearing that I had not read those submissions, which were not filed in compliance with the orders made 5 December 2025.
Counsel for the wife informed the Court that the second respondent’s submissions sought to re-agitate the question as to whether the Final Orders did in fact finalise the proceeding; he submitted that in circumstances where that issue had been resolved by the High Court in Genesalio, he did not intend to respond to those late-filed submissions.
In light of that submission, I sought clarification from counsel for the second respondent as to whether he sought to re-agitate that issue. I confirmed that the issue had been previously raised before me as well as before the High Court and that the High Court had concluded the issue. Counsel for the second respondent confirmed his understanding of that position. Counsel for the second respondent confirmed that he did not draft the submissions filed and that he could not speak to those submissions; he noted that the submissions agitate the question of the regularity of the orders made and confirmed that he did not propose to make submissions as to those matters. Counsel confirmed there was nothing further he could submit with respect to those matters and that he did not intend to do so. The matter therefore proceeded on that basis.
Notwithstanding that concession, at the conclusion of his oral submissions, counsel for the second respondent submitted that the second respondent did seek to rely on his written submissions. Counsel again confirmed that he would not speak to those submissions but maintained that the second respondent sought that the Court consider those matters.
I noted that the second respondent was, in effect, seeking to re-agitate matters previously ventilated before and determined by the High Court. The second respondent’s counsel conceded that this was so.
Having regard to that concession, and in circumstances where those matters have been determined by the High Court of Australia, I do not intend to consider those matters further. The second respondent’s application with respect to the circumstances in which the Final Orders were made has been heard and determined. Plainly, the second respondent is dissatisfied and does not accept that decision. However, this Court is bound by that decision. It is not open to the second respondent to seek to again ventilate that issue; to do so would visit a significant injustice upon the wife, who has endured protracted proceedings in this Court and the High Court of Australia, and is entitled to the fruits of the Final Orders. The second respondent’s pursuit of what is a dead issue serves only to increase the wife’s legal costs and further deplete already stretched court resources. That application will be dismissed. The second respondent’s pursuit of the matter will otherwise be considered in the context of the wife’s application for costs.

Should the wife be appointed as attorney to conduct the default sales?

The wife seeks orders that she be appointed attorney for each of the respondents in respect of the default sales provided for by Orders 9 and 10 of the Final Orders. Further, she seeks an order that she have sole conduct of the default sales to the exclusion of the respondents and that she be authorised to provide instructions on behalf of she and the respondents (as applicable) in relation to the conduct, terms and conditions of those sales.
Orders 9 and 10 of the Final Orders provide as follows:-
That in default of the Payment (save for where the Wife has failed to comply with order 2 hereof):
within 21 days the Husband and the Second to Seventh Respondents (as applicable) do all such acts and things and sign all such documents to cause the following properties to be sold (Default Sales) within 60 days;
(a) if the Payment outstanding (plus interest as applicable pursuant to order 9(b)(c) hereof) is less than $1,665,000 of the Payment is owing, [3 G Street] property;

(b) if the Payment outstanding (plus interest as applicable pursuant to order 9(b)(c) hereof) is more than $1,665,000 but less than $2,570,000 of the Payment is owing, [3 G Street] and [1 G Street] property be sold together (so as to enable both to be sold to one third party), or as otherwise recommended by the selling agent;

(c) if the Payment outstanding (plus interest as applicable pursuant to order 9(b)(c) hereof) is more than $2,570,000 of the Payment is owing the following properties be sold:

(i) [3 G Street] and [1 G Street] be sold together (so as to enable both to be sold to one third party), or as otherwise recommended by the selling agent;
(ii) [F Street]

(iii) [2 G Street]; and

(iv) [H Street].
b. the proceeds of the Default Sales be applied:
(a) firstly, to the sale costs, commissions and expenses of the sale and the selling agents;

(b) secondly, to discharge the mortgage relating to that property; and

(c) thirdly, to the Wife in the sum of the Payment or such amount thereof as remains outstanding, plus interest applied at the rate prescribed for the applicable period in the Federal Circuit and Family Court Rules 2021 (Cth) from the due date for the Payment pursuant to clause 1 hereof to the date the Payment is made in full; and

(d) the balance to the registered proprietor (or their nominee).
As to the Default Sales, unless otherwise agreed between the Wife, the Husband and [Mr M Genesalio] in writing, the terms and conditions of the Sales include:
(a) an agent be appointed as follows:
(i) in relation to the [3 G Street] property, within 7 days of default, [Mr M Genesalio] propose three selling agents, from which the Wife is free to choose one, and in the absence of agreement an agent be appointed as nominated by the Real Estate institute of Victoria and

(ii) in relation to any of the other properties to be sold, [Mr M Genesalio] is to appoint and agent and advise the Husband and the Wife within 7 days of such appointment;

(b) the former matrimonial home and [1 G Street] be sold together as a parcel of land in the circumstances described above unless otherwise recommended by the agent;

(c) each of the Sales be by public auction unless otherwise recommended by the agent;

(d) the reserve prices for the Sales be as follows

(i) the former matrimonial home, if permitted to be sold alone, be $1,715,000;

(ii) the former matrimonial home and [1 G Street], sold together, have a reserve price set at $3,150,000;

(iii) [2 G Street] have a reserve price set at $1,715,000;

(iv) [F street] have a reserve price set at $920,000;

(v) [H Street] have a reserve price set at $600,000;

(e) If a property fails to sell at public auction for a price at or above the reserve price, the property be listed for sale at a price as nominated by the selling agent in order to obtain the highest sale price possible, the parties having the opportunity to inform the agent of their preferred sale price, but the ultimate discretion as to price being determined by the selling agent.

(f) absent agreement within 14 days of default, the conveyancer be appointed by the Law Institute of Victoria and the parties jointly instruct the conveyancer for the Sales; and

(g) liberty be reserved to the parties to apply with respect to the terms and conditions of the sale in default of agreement.

In support of the orders sought, it was submitted that in circumstances where the Final Orders had been made approximately 16 months ago, and those orders were confirmed by the High Court of Australia by orders made 28 November 2025, the wife was entitled to enforcement of her entitlements pursuant to those orders. It was submitted that the non-compliance of the husband and the second respondent with the Final Orders was ongoing and necessitated the relief sought by the wife. In support of that position, it was submitted that as at the date of hearing on 4 February 2026:-
(a) The payment due to the wife pursuant to Order 1 of the Final Orders remained outstanding. As at 30 January 2026 the amount due to the wife pursuant to the Orders had increased to $4,417,470.25. That amount does not include costs of the High Court proceedings.

(b) Notwithstanding orders restraining the respondents from dealing with property other than for the purposes of financing the payment to the wife (Order 3(a) of the Final Orders), on 6 January 2026 a caveat was registered on the title to the property at 1 G Street, Suburb C which is registered in the name of the second respondent. That caveat records that the caveator asserts an interest in the property pursuant to an agreement with the registered proprietor dated 11 December 2025 which granted the caveator an interest as chargee.[1]

(c) The agreement referred to in that caveat has not been produced to the wife, notwithstanding requests for the same.

(d) The keys to the properties the subject of the default sale orders were not provided to the appointed selling agents until the Friday before the listed hearing, and only in circumstances where the wife’s lawyers had threatened to bring an application in contempt.

(e) In December 2025, the selling agent arranged for advertising boards to be placed in front of the three properties at 3 G Street, 1 G Street and 2 G Street, Suburb C. The second respondent was informed of the placement of those boards by email from the selling agent to him dated 17 December 2025. On 9 January 2026, the wife notified her lawyers that the auction board for the property at 2 G Street had been removed and placed behind locked gates at that property, face down.[2]

(f) The selling agent had still not had access to the properties to be sold by 15 January 2026. Further, the selling agent had yet to be provided with vendor statements and contracts for sale in respect of the subject properties.[3]

It is against that backdrop that the wife seeks orders that she be appointed as attorney to conduct the sales. It was submitted on her behalf that any cooperation provided by the husband or his brother was only made at the eleventh hour, and in the face of threats to bring a contempt application. Having regard to the chronology detailed above, I accept that submission.
It was submitted on behalf of the second respondent that the appointment of the wife as attorney to conduct the sales was unnecessary as he had been making “strong efforts” to comply with the Final Orders. I do not accept that submission in circumstances where the Final Orders were made in August 2024 and the non-compliance by the husband and the second respondent has necessitated two separate interlocutory applications by the wife, in July 2025 and October 2025, to action the default provisions in those orders.
That the Court has power to enforce its orders is not in issue. That power extends to the power to make injunctive orders in aid of the enforcement of an obligation, per Rule 11.07 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”). Further, s 114(3) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) provides that the Court may grant an injunction by interlocutory order or otherwise, including an injunction in aid of the enforcement of the decree in any case in which it appears to the Court to be just or convenient to do so and either unconditionally or upon such terms and conditions as the Court considers appropriate.
I am satisfied that both the husband and the second respondent have ignored their obligations under the Final Orders with respect to the default sales. They have also disregarded their obligations pursuant to Order 1(b) of the Orders made 3 July 2025 which required them to comply with any reasonable request of the selling agent appointed to conduct the sale of any of the properties within 24 hours of receipt of such request or such other time as required by the selling agent.
Any co-operation from the husband and the second respondent has only been provided late in the day, after the wife has been forced to file applications to enforce the Final Orders, and in the case of the provision of keys to the selling agents, at the threshold of the court door on the Friday immediately preceding this hearing.
Having regard to that history, this Court has no confidence that the husband or the second respondent will comply with court orders regarding the default sales; at every stage since the Final Orders were made, they have failed to comply with orders or cooperate with the sales process. Given that position, I am satisfied that it is both just and convenient that the wife be appointed as attorney for the default sales. To that end I have made orders appointing the wife as attorney for the default sales, granting her sole conduct of the sales to the exclusion of the respondents, and authorised the appointed selling agent to affix signage, enter and remain at the properties for the purpose of conducting the sales and permit members of the public access to the properties for the purposes of the sales.

Should the asset preservation order continue?

The wife seeks orders restraining the respondents from transferring or encumbering specified properties until such time as she has received payment in full of her entitlements pursuant to the Final Orders. As noted earlier, at the time of hearing the wife’s entitlement to payment pursuant to the Final Orders exceeded $4,400,000.
That the Court has the power to make the order sought by the wife is not in question. That this is so was confirmed by Aldridge J in Sresbodan & Sresbodan [2016] FamCA 954 at [105].
As was observed by Kent J in Worth & Worth (No.2) [2020] FamCA 228 at [55] the power to make such an order for asset preservation goes beyond the legislative grant of power under s 114 of the Act. In support of that position his Honour referred to the decision of the High Court in Jackson v Sterling Industries Limited [1987] HCA 23; (1987) 162 CLR 612 at [623] where Dean J (with whom Mason CJ, Wilson, Brennan and Dawson JJ agreed) stated:-
That general power [to grant an asset preservation order] should, however, now be accepted as an established part of the armoury of a court of law and equity to prevent the abuse or frustration of its process in relation to matters coming within its jurisdiction.

...

Indeed, even in the absence of the provisions of s 23, the Federal Court would have possessed power to make such orders in relation to matters properly before it, as an incident of the general grant to it as a superior court of law and equity of the jurisdiction to deal with such matters.
The High Court was there dealing with the powers of the Federal Court of Australia in relation to the grant of an asset preservation order.
The principles in relation to the making of interlocutory injunctions are well established and have been summarised by the Full Court in Tsiang & Wu [2019] FamCAFC 128; (2019) FLC 93-911 (“Tsiang”). At [20] to [21] the Full Court confirmed the test that must be satisfied by an applicant seeking such an order as follows:-
...An applicant must demonstrate first that there is a serious issue to be tried. While that statement has been the subject of various iterations, in essence it requires the demonstration of an arguable case or as was said in Australian Broadcasting Corporation v O’Neill [2006] HCA 46; (2006) 227 CLR 57 at [65] , the applicant must “show a sufficient likelihood of success to justify in the circumstances the preservation of the status quo”.

Next the applicant must demonstrate that the balance of convenience favours making the order sought. As part of this, the applicant must show that there is a “danger” or risk of dissipation of or dealings with assets which will frustrate any judgment in favour of the applicant.
Whether a prima facie case has been established and whether the balance of convenience favours the granting of interlocutory relief are related questions; it may be necessary to examine the strength of a party’s case to determine the risk of injustice which may be visited upon either party by the granting or withholding of interlocutory relief (Warner-Lambert Co LCC v Apotex Pty Ltd [2014] FCAFC 59; (2014) 311 ALR 632 at [70]).
It is not necessary to establish a positive intention on the part of the respondent to deal with assets in a way which will frustrate judgment in favour of the applicant. A mere possibility of such dealings occurring, as inferred by the facts and circumstances established by the applicant’s evidence, is sufficient (Tsiang at [25]; Mullen and De Bry [2006] FamCA 1380; (2006) FLC 93-293 at [49]). However, the risk of dissipation must be real, and not theoretical (Tsiang at [27]). The wife submits that she has a prima facie case for the injunction as she has an entitlement to the payment under the Final Orders, as well as to interest accrued thereon. I accept that submission.
Prior to the filing of the current application, on 12 June 2025 the wife filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking the respondents’ compliance with the default provisions under the Final Orders. On 16 June 2025, orders were made by consent regarding the appointment of selling agents and a conveyancer; the wife’s application for injunctions against the respondents from interfering with the default sales was opposed by them. On 3 July 2025, orders were made restraining the husband and the second respondent from interfering with the default sales and requiring them to comply with any reasonable request of the selling agents.
Those orders were made in circumstances where both the husband and the second respondent failed to comply with their obligations under the Final Orders.
It was submitted on behalf of the wife that having regard to the respondents’ now long history of non-compliance with the Final Orders, it is reasonable to anticipate that there will be further obstruction by them with respect to the sales. Whilst the wife awaits payment of her entitlements, default interest is continuing to accrue and as already observed, the amount payable to the wife now is in excess of $4,400,000.
Again, having regard to the conduct identified above, there is much force in the submission made that the balance of convenience favours the wife with respect to her application for an asset preservation order. I am well satisfied that there is a real likelihood that the disruptive behaviour of the husband and the second respondent with respect to their obligations pursuant to the Final Orders will continue. That view is bolstered having regard to the recent conduct of the second respondent to enter into an agreement with a third party on 11 December 2025 without notice to the wife, the effect of which was to entitle that third party as chargee to lodge a caveat against the title to the property at 2 G Street. That action was taken by the second respondent in the face of an order that he be restrained from transferring, encumbering or further encumbering the property pending payment to the wife.[4]
Further, I am satisfied that the wife’s efforts to enforce her rights under the Final Orders have been frustrated at every stage by the actions of the respondents, particularly the second respondent. That too, was the view of the High Court of Australia in Genesalio (supra) where it assessed the respondent’s application for a constitutional writ seeking to quash the Final orders as follows:-
...The unavoidable inference from the evidence was that the plaintiffs had commenced the proceeding in this Court only after they had failed to stymie compliance with the final Orders, made by consent on the basis of legal advice, in the Division 1 Court. Moreover, the plaintiffs had done so in circumstances where they had not sought to appeal the final orders in the Division 1 Court. The further unavoidable inference from the evidence was that the plaintiffs were using the application to this Court to avoid the consequences of the decision they had made based on legal advice to settle the proceedings in the Division 1 Court.
I respectfully share the views expressed by the High Court of Australia.
The wife seeks orders to restrain the respondents from dealing with the properties at L Street Suburb C (“[L Street]”) and 1 D Street Suburb C (“[D Street]”) in addition to the properties referred to in Order 3 of the Final Orders.
That application is opposed by the husband and the second respondent. They submit that there is no risk of dissipation of assets.
The second respondent also submits that the properties sought to be the subject of the injunctions are not matrimonial property but are properties are held by third parties. I do not accept that submission in circumstances where the title to L Street is registered in the name of the fourth respondent and D Street is registered in the name of the fifth respondent and Order 1 of the Final Orders provides that the respondents (which includes the fourth and fifth respondents) be jointly and severally liable for payment to the wife of the sum of $4,000,000.
The injunctions are sought in circumstances where the anticipated proceeds of the default sale properties are estimated to be approximately $4,019,189,[5] whilst the wife’s entitlements to payment pursuant to the Final Orders plus interest and the costs orders made 10 December 2025 total $4,352,587[6] as at 4 February 2026. Interest continues to accrue on the outstanding payment of $4,000,000 pursuant to Order 1. In addition, the wife seeks costs on an indemnity basis of and incidental to this application as well as with respect to her application made pursuant to s 106A of the Act, that claim totalling approximately $115,000.
The wife submits that in circumstances where there is likely to a shortfall between her entitlements under the Final Orders, which are ever-increasing, and the anticipated net proceeds of sale of the default sale properties, the asset preservation orders are necessary to ensure that the respondents are able to satisfy her entitlements under the Final Orders. She further submits that given the conduct of the husband and the second respondent since the Final Orders were made, there is a real risk that they will dissipate assets so as to frustrate satisfaction of her entitlements under the Final Orders.
In support of that submission, she relies upon the following conduct of the husband and/or the second respondent:-
(a) On 25 June 2025, without notice to the wife, a new mortgage was registered over the title to the L Street property (registered in the name of the fourth respondent) in the sum of $2,800,000;[7]

(b) On 17 November 2025, approximately one month after the wife’s application for the asset preservation orders had been filed and served, without notice to the wife the second and fourth respondents caused a further mortgage in the sum of $80,000 to be registered over the title to L Street;[8]

(c) On 11 December 2025 the second respondent entered into an agreement, without notice to the wife, which granted a third party an interest in G Street as chargee, and resulted in a caveat being lodged over the title to that property on 6 January 2026. That agreement was executed notwithstanding the order restraining the second respondent from further encumbering the property pending full payment to the wife of her entitlements under the Final Orders.

(d) On 21 January 2026 the wife caused her lawyer to send an email to the second respondent’s solicitor seeking information regarding that caveat. As at the date of hearing, no response to that request had been provided on behalf of the second respondent,[9] and the second respondent has adduced no evidence in relation to the agreement or the caveat.

I am satisfied that the conduct relied upon by the wife supports the making of an asset preservation order; the risk of the respondents dissipating assets is real having regard to that conduct. Since the Final Orders were made the second respondent has dealt with property the subject of those Orders, and the restraints imposed by them, at will, and without reference or notice to the wife. The second respondent’s counsel sought to justify such behaviour, submitting that the charge over G Street was for only $1,100. There is no evidence before the Court which confirms that position and even were it so, that does not excuse or justify what is a plain breach of an order of this Court.
Having regard to those matters, I have no confidence that the husband and the second respondent will desist in their endeavours to obstruct the default sales and thwart the wife’s attempts to obtain her entitlements under the Final Orders. Further, I am satisfied that there is a real risk that the respondents will dissipate assets which will frustrate the wife’s ability to receive her entitlements pursuant to the Final Orders. Given that position, I am satisfied that it is necessary that asset preservation orders be made restraining the respondents from dealing with the properties at D Street, L Street and H Street until such time as the wife has received payment of the amounts due to her pursuant to the Final Orders.

Should the respondents pay the wife’s costs arising from her application to enforce the Final Orders?

The wife seeks that the respondents be jointly and severally liable to pay her indemnity costs of and incidental to the current application fixed in the sum of $103,155.96 together with indemnity costs relating to her application pursuant to Order 16 of the Final Orders for the execution of the agreements with the selling agents and conveyancers by a Registrar of the Court , fixed in the sum of $12,330.74, a total of $115,486.70. In the alternative, she seeks her costs calculated in accordance with Schedule 3 of the Rules, fixed in the sum of $87,201.79.

LEGAL PRINCIPLES

The costs provisions under the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) were amended by the Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth) ("the Amendment Act") which came into effect as and from 10 June 2025. The effect of the amendment was to repeal s 117 of the Act and to implement Part XIVC of the Act, which at s 114UB specifically deals with costs orders.
The first question for determination is whether the provisions implemented pursuant to the Amendment Act apply to these proceedings. The relevant transitional provisions are set out in the Amendment Act at Schedule 4, Item 15 which provides as follows:-
The amendments of the Family Law Act 1975 made by Divisions 1 and 2 of this Part apply in relation to the following proceedings:
(a) proceedings instituted on or after the day this Division commences;

(b) proceedings instituted before, and not finally determined before, the day this Division commences, other than proceedings in respect of which a final hearing has commenced before that day.

Section 4(1) of the Act provides that "proceedings" are defined as follows:-
...
(b) otherwise-means a proceeding in a court, whether between parties or not, and includes cross-proceedings or an incidental proceeding in the course of or in connection with a proceeding.
As the Final Orders were made prior to the Amendment Act coming into effect, the proceedings under the Act were finally determined. The wife's Enforcement Application seeks to enforce the provisions of the Final Orders and is therefore an incidental proceeding in connection with the original proceedings under Part VIII of the Act. Having regard to those circumstances, I am satisfied that the relevant provisions as to costs for the purposes of this application are those that were in effect prior to 10 June 2025. Accordingly, it is an application to be determined pursuant to s 117 of the Act.
Section 117 of the Act provides that each party to proceedings is to bear their own costs unless there are circumstances which in the opinion of the Court justify the making of a costs order.
Section 117(2) of the Act provides that:-
(2) If, in proceedings under this Act, the court is of opinion that there are circumstances that justify it in doing so, the court may, subject to subsections (2A), (4), (4A), (5) and (6) and the applicable Rules of Court, make such order as to costs and security for costs, whether by way of interlocutory order or otherwise, as the court considers just.
Section 117(2A) of the Act provides that in determining what, if any, order should be made under subsection (2), the Court must have regard to the following:-
(a) the financial circumstances of each of the parties to the proceedings;

(b) whether any party to the proceedings is in receipt of assistance by way of legal aid and, if so, the terms of the grant of that assistance to that party;

(c) the conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings including, without limiting the generality of the foregoing, the conduct of the parties in relation to pleadings, particulars, discovery, inspection, directions to answer questions, admissions of facts, production of documents and similar matters;

(d) whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the court;

(e) whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings;

(f) whether either party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle the proceedings and the terms of any such offer; and

(g) such other matters as the court considers relevant.

Should it be determined that it is just to make an order as to costs, costs are not awarded to punish the unsuccessful party, but rather, are compensatory in the sense that they are awarded to ameliorate the expense of the successful party resulting from them having been required to participate in the proceedings.
The discretion afforded by s 117 of the Act is broad and the relevant factors contained in s 117(2A) are not to be read in a restrictive way; any one of those factors may found an order for costs, but all factors must be considered and balanced (I and I [1995] FamCA 80; (1995) FLC 92-625).
The Court has a broad discretion as to the weight to be attributed to the factors set out in s 117(2A) (Medlon & Medlon (No. 6) (Indemnity Costs) [2015] FamCAFC 157; (2015) FLC 93-664 at [24]).
The method of calculation of costs is prescribed by r 12.17(1) of the Rules, which provides:-
(1) The court may order that a party is entitled to costs:
(a) of a specific amount; or

(b) as assessed on a particular basis (for example, party and party, solicitor and client or indemnity); or

(c) to be calculated in accordance with the method stated in the order; or

(d) for part of the proceeding, or part of an amount, assessed in accordance with Schedule 3.

Rule 12.17(3) sets out matters that may be considered in the calculation of costs, providing that:-
(3) In making an order under subrule (1), the court may consider the following:
(a) the importance, complexity or difficulty of the issues;

(b) the reasonableness of each party's behaviour in the proceeding including by having regard to the matters set out in subrule 12.08(2);

(c) the rates ordinarily payable to lawyers in comparable proceedings;

(d) whether a lawyer's conduct has been improper, unfair, unreasonable or disproportionate;

(e) the time properly spent on the proceedings, or in complying with pre-action procedures;

(f) whether expenses (paid or payable) are fair, reasonable and proportionate.

Having regard to the above matters, I consider that I hold a broad discretion in respect of matters relating to any costs orders.
The law with respect to indemnity costs is well settled; the Full Court observed in Kohan & Kohan [1992] FamCA 116; (1993) FLC 92-340 that the Court should not depart lightly from the ordinary rules with respect to costs. In the decision of Prantage & Prantage [2013] FamCAFC 105; (2013) 49 Fam LR 197, the Full Court confirmed that indemnity costs should only be awarded if the case has some special or unusual feature.
In Colgate-Palmolive v Cussons Pty Ltd [1993] FCA 536; (1993) 46 FCR 225, Sheppard J identified circumstances that might constitute special or unusual features so as to justify an award of indemnity costs. Usefully, Holden CJ in Munday v Bowman (1997) FLC 92-784 at 84,660 drew from the decision of Sheppard J the following examples:-
(a) Where it appears that an action has been commenced or continued in circumstances where a party properly advised should have known that he had no chance of success. In such cases the action must be presumed to have been commenced or continued for some ulterior motive or because of some wilful disregard of the known facts.

(b) Making allegations of fraud, knowing them to be false, and the making of irrelevant allegations of fraud.

(c) Evidence of particular misconduct causing loss of time to the court and to other parties.

(d) The making of allegations which ought never to have been made or the undue prolongation of a case by groundless contentions.

(e) An imprudent refusal of an offer to compromise.

(Citations omitted)
The category of circumstances which enliven the discretion to award indemnity costs are not closed; the circumstances do not need to come precisely within the examples provided by Sheppard J.
I now turn to the considerations set out at s 117(2A) of the Act.

Section 117(2A)(a) – the financial circumstances of the parties

None of the parties made submissions with respect to their financial circumstances.
The Final Orders make provision for the payment of $4,000,000 to the wife. That sum, together with interest thereon, remains outstanding. The second to seventh respondents are jointly and severally liable for the payment to be made to the wife. The Final Orders also provide for the respondents to retain their interests in real properties as identified in the Final Orders. Having regard to the terms of the Final Orders, which were made by consent, I am satisfied that the respondents have significant property and financial resources at their disposal from which they can meet an order for costs.
In any event, it is well-settled that impecuniosity is not a bar to the making of an order for costs (Lenova & Lenova (Costs) [2011] FamCAFC 141).

Section 117 (2A)(b) – whether any party to the proceeding is receiving assistance by way of legal aid in respect of the proceedings

None of the parties in this dispute are in receipt of assistance by way of legal aid.

Section 117(2A)(c) – the conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings

The wife’s Amended Application in a Proceeding has been filed in circumstances where the husband and the second respondent have failed to comply with their obligations under the Final Orders. It is against that backdrop that the wife has been required to seek orders that she be appointed as attorney to conduct the default sales and further that there be orders for asset preservation pending receipt by her of full payment of her entitlements under the Final Orders.
I refer to my earlier findings that the husband and the second respondent have not complied with the Final Orders. They have not made the payment to the wife as required by Order 1 of the Final Orders. Further, they have not complied with their obligations pursuant to Orders 9 and 10 of the Final Orders regarding the default sales. It is due to their persistent non-compliance with those orders that this application has been necessitated.
At every stage of the implementation of the Final Orders there has been neglect or obstruction by the husband and the second respondent. Orders have had to be made for the appointment of selling agents and conveyancers. Further orders have been made requiring the husband and the second respondent to cooperate with the selling agent and the conveyancer. The matter comes before the Court in circumstances where the keys to the properties to be sold were only delivered to the selling agents by the husband and/or the second respondent on the Friday immediately preceding commencement of the hearing. Moreover, there has been non-compliance with orders restraining the husband and the second respondent from dealing with the subject properties. As recently as 11 December 2025 the second respondent granted a charge against the title to 2 G Street, notwithstanding that order.
I am satisfied that the conduct of the husband and the second respondent has been obstructive and has thwarted the satisfaction of the Final Orders, thus depriving the wife of the fruits of those Orders. It is conduct which has unnecessarily delayed the proceedings and served to increase the costs incurred by the wife.
I am satisfied that that conduct is a significant factor in the determination of the wife’s application for costs.

Section 117(2A)(d) – whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the Court

As recorded earlier in this judgment, these proceedings have wholly been necessitated due to the failure of the husband and the second respondent to comply with the terms of the Final Orders, particularly the requirement for them to make a payment pursuant to Order 1 and to effect the default sales as required by Orders 9 and 10.

Section 117(2A)(e) – whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings

The husband and the second respondent were wholly unsuccessful in opposing the wife’s application that she be appointed as attorney for the default sales and that there be orders for asset preservation.

Section 117(2A)(f) – whether either party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to settle the proceedings

No submissions were made with respect to this consideration.

CONCLUSION

Having regard to the above findings I am satisfied that there are circumstances that justify an order that the husband and the second respondent be jointly and severally liable for the payment of the wife’s costs of and incidental to the proceeding.

Should costs be awarded on an indemnity basis?

The costs agreement as between the wife and her lawyer is annexed to the affidavit of Mr CC filed 30 June 2025. That agreement dated 4 June 2018 together with an updating disclosure statement dated 4 June 2025 are both annexed to that affidavit. Having regard to that evidence, I am satisfied that the wife has complied with her obligations pursuant to r 12.13(4) of the Rules.
Mr CC deposes as to the wife’s costs of and incidental to the current application at paragraph 36 of his affidavit filed 30 January 2026. There was no challenge to the quantum of costs calculated by him in accordance with the costs agreement.
I have found the husband and the second respondent have behaved in a manner which has had the effect of thwarting the operation of the Final Orders. In doing so, they have obstructed the wife from receiving her entitlements under the Final Orders.
In addition, they have filed proceedings in the High Court of Australia seeking to quash the Final Orders, that application filed some 8 months after the orders were made by consent and in circumstances where there was no appeal against them. That application was assessed by the High Court as an act designed to “stymie compliance with the Final Orders made by consent on the basis of legal advice”. Further, that Court found that “the unavoidable inference from the evidence was that [the husband and the second respondent] were using the application to [the High Court of Australia] to avoid the consequences of the decision they had made based on legal advice to settle the proceedings in [this Court]”. I respectfully agree with the inferences drawn by the High Court of Australia as to the conduct of the husband and the second respondent.
Having regard to all of those matters, I am satisfied that those circumstances justify an order for costs in favour of the wife on an indemnity basis. I am satisfied that conduct is of an exceptional nature, designed to frustrate the operation of the Final Orders and deprive the wife of her entitlements under those orders.
The amount claimed by the wife with respect to this application is substantial. However, a review of the Schedule of Costs pertaining to this application[10] identifies the voluminous amount of correspondence received by her lawyers from the second respondent personally, as well as from the lawyers on the record representing him. As a result of that conduct, the wife’s legal costs have been substantially increased. I am satisfied that the respondents ought be jointly and severally liable for those costs.
Having regard to all of the above matters, I am satisfied that the husband and the second respondent ought be jointly and severally liable for payment of the wife’s costs of and incidental to the Amended Application in a Proceeding filed 2 December 2025 calculated on an indemnity basis and fixed in the sum of $103,155.96.
As to the costs incurred by the wife as a result of her application pursuant to Order 16 of the Final Orders, Mr CC deposes as to those costs at paragraph 43 of his affidavit affirmed on 19 September 2025 and filed on 4 November 2025. The quantum of those costs is contained in the Schedule of Costs annexed to that affidavit.[11]
Again, there was no challenge to the quantum of costs as calculated in accordance with the costs agreement. A review of that schedule confirms the work and attendances undertaken by the wife’s lawyers in order to enforce the Final Orders. In circumstances where that work has also been necessitated as a result of the non-compliance of the husband and the second respondent with the Final Orders requiring them to execute documents to affect the default sales, I am satisfied that they ought meet the costs of that application on an indemnity basis. Accordingly, I will order that they be jointly and severally liable for payment of those costs fixed in the sum of $12,330.74.
